languageExamples.description: A common example of epenthesis in English where a stop is inserted after a nasal and before a voiceless fricative, particularly when a morpheme boundary is present or perceived. For instance, 'prince' /prɪns/ is often pronounced [prɪnts] and 'sense' /sɛns/ as [sɛnts].

sources: Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
preamble: Affrication of the alveolar nasal [n] to the voiceless alveolar affricate [ts], often through an epenthetic process. A stop [t] develops between the nasal and a following fricative [s], and the original nasal may be lost.
